As Angular applications grow, architecture becomes far more important than syntax. At a small scale, almost any codebase can work. A few components, a couple of services, and everything feels manageable, but once an application grows — more features, more developers, more business requirements — things start to change.
You begin to notice common problems:

• Components become huge

• Services grow uncontrollably

• Logic gets duplicated

• Features become tightly coupled

• Testing becomes painful

• Small changes break unrelated parts of the application
If you’ve worked on large Angular projects, you’ve probably seen at least a few of these. This is exactly where good design principles become essential.
One of the most valuable sets of design principles in software engineering is SOLID.
If you’re preparing for senior Angular interviews, expect SOLID to come up often. But beyond interviews, these principles genuinely help you build better software. First of all, we need to break SOLID down and understand what it means. SOLID stands for:

• S → Single Responsibility Principle

• O → Open/Closed Principle

• L → Liskov Substitution Principle

• I → Interface Segregation Principle

• D → Dependency Inversion Principle
In this series, I’ll break down each SOLID principle from an Angular engineer’s perspective — with practical examples and real-world Angular use cases.
Each upcoming article will focus on one principle and show how it applies to modern Angular applications:

• Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) → How to keep components clean and focused

• Open/Closed Principle (OCP) → How Angular’s dependency injection helps build extensible systems

• Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) → Why overusing inheritance can hurt Angular architecture

• Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) → Why smaller contracts lead to better maintainability

• Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) → The architectural power behind Angular’s DI system
If you’re building Angular applications at scale — or preparing for senior-level frontend interviews — this series will help you think more deeply about software design and architecture. Let’s dive into this series with the first article, which is the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) in Angular
